coredata.dc:title: Morus nigra-derived hydrophilic carbon dots for the highly selective and sensitive detection of ferric ion in aqueous media and human colon cancer cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Perumal, Suguna
Vinodh, Rajangam
Sundramoorthy, Ashok K.
Babu, Rajendran Suresh
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: Strongly fluorescent hydrophilic carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal route using polysaccharide-rich Morus nigra fruit as the carbon source. These Morus nigra CDs (M-CDs) were extensively characterized using various analytical techniques to determine their structural and optical properties. The as-synthesized M-CDs were determined to be monodispersed with a mean diameter of 4.5 nm. Aqueous M-CD dispersions are brownish-yellow in color in daylight and emit bright cyan-blue light upon exposure to ultraviolet light. The M-CDs exhibit typical excitation-dependent emission behavior, with a high quantum yield of 24%. The analytical data show that the as-synthesized M-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence (FLR) and are very optically stable. Hence, the as-synthesized M-CDs are expected to use as fluorescent sensors for the highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions by the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The quenching rate was linearly dependent on Fe3+ concentration, with a Fe3+ detection limit of 0.47 μM and a 5–30 µm detection range. Moreover, human colon cancer (HTC-116) cells were stained with the prepared M-CDs for 12 and 24 h for cell viability and microscopic analyses. The M-CDs-conjugated HTC-116 cells were brightly multicolor illuminated, emitting blue, green, and red light when excited through 405-, 488-, and 555-nm filters with distinct excitation ranges, respectively. This study provides a new route for the large-scale synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs for diverse applications using green sources and one-step hydrothermal carbonization procedures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Morus nigra
Hydrothermal method
Carbon dot
Fluorescence sensor
Fluorescence quenching
Bio-imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Applying the synthesized M-CDs to HCT-116 cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of the prepared M-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of the prepared M-CDs. High-resolution (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s XPS spectra of the M-CDs.
(a) FESEM image of the prepared M-CDs and the corresponding (b) carbon, (c) nitrogen, (d) oxygen, and (e) overlapping elemental maps of the presented elementals.
(a–c) HRTEM image of the prepared M-CDs (Inset in panel (a): M-CD particle-size histogram).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum (inset: photographic images of aqueous M-CD solutions exposed to daylight (left) and 360 nm UV light (right)). (b) FLR emission spectra of the prepared M-CDs were recorded with 10-nm excitation-wavelength increments in the 300–460 nm range.
FLR emission spectra of the prepared M-CDs at (a) different storage times and (b) different irradiation times (irradiated with a 4-W 365-nm UV light).

(a) FLR emission spectra of the prepared M-CDs in the presence of various metal ions (1000 μM) in aqueous solution and (b) the corresponding FLR responses. (c) M-CDs FLR quenching with increasing Fe3+ concentrations (0–30 μM). (d) Relative M-CDs FLR response (F0/F) as a function of Fe3+ concentration (0–30 μM).
Photographic images of aqueous M-CD solutions containing various metal ions (1000 μM) under (a) daylight and (b) UV light.
Plausible M-CD FLR quenching mechanism in the presence of Fe3+.
Cell viabilities of HCT-116 (human colon cancer) cells in the presence of various concentrations (0–200 mg mL−1) of the prepared M-CDs.

FLR images of HCT-116 (human colon cancer) cells incubated with M-CDs and M-CDs+Fe3+ using filters with excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, and 555 nm, and in a bright field; M-CD concentration: 100 μg mL−1.
Depicting the synthesis of FL M-CDs from Morus nigra fruit using a one-step hydrothermal route.
Comparing various FL nanosensors from natural sources for the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous media.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI): Materials, instrumentation methods, quantum yield measurements, sensing of metal ions, photobleaching stability, prolonged stability, cell culture, cell viability assay, fluorescence microscopy, deconvoluted Raman spectrum, FESEM images, fluorescence excitation spectrum, and fluorescence emission normalized spectra of the obtained M-CDs.

head.ce:title: Morus nigra-derived hydrophilic carbon dots for the highly selective and sensitive detection of ferric ion in aqueous media and human colon cancer cell imaging

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Strongly fluorescent hydrophilic carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal route using polysaccharide-rich Morus nigra fruit as the carbon source. These Morus nigra CDs (M-CDs) were extensively characterized using various analytical techniques to determine their structural and optical properties. The as-synthesized M-CDs were determined to be monodispersed with a mean diameter of 4.5 nm. Aqueous M-CD dispersions are brownish-yellow in color in daylight and emit bright cyan-blue light upon exposure to ultraviolet light. The M-CDs exhibit typical excitation-dependent emission behavior, with a high quantum yield of 24%. The analytical data show that the as-synthesized M-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence (FLR) and are very optically stable. Hence, the as-synthesized M-CDs are expected to use as fluorescent sensors for the highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions by the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The quenching rate was linearly dependent on Fe3+ concentration, with a Fe3+ detection limit of 0.47 μM and a 5–30 µm detection range. Moreover, human colon cancer (HTC-116) cells were stained with the prepared M-CDs for 12 and 24 h for cell viability and microscopic analyses. The M-CDs-conjugated HTC-116 cells were brightly multicolor illuminated, emitting blue, green, and red light when excited through 405-, 488-, and 555-nm filters with distinct excitation ranges, respectively. This study provides a new route for the large-scale synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs for diverse applications using green sources and one-step hydrothermal carbonization procedures.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are novel carbon nanomaterials; they are almost spherical nanoclusters less than 10 nm in size and are considered to be composed of crystalline or amorphous cores with predominantly sp2 carbon and abundant functional groups on their shells [1–4]. CDs have attracted considerable attention over the past decade for diverse applications, including bio-labeling, photocatalysis, fluorescence (FLR) inks, sensing, and biomedical applications, which is attributed to their excellent optical properties, including tunable FLR behavior, chemical inertness, good biodegradability, low toxicity, good water dispersibility, environmental friendliness, ease of production, and resistance to photobleaching [5–10]. CDs are most commonly prepared using top-down and bottom-up approaches [11]. In the former, which includes laser ablation, acid hydrolysis, discharge, and electrochemical oxidation, CDs are prepared from bulk carbon materials, including graphite, carbon nanotubes, and carbon black [12]. However, such top-down methods require costly apparatus and resources, as well as complicated settings. In contrast, CDs are synthesized from natural sources and inexpensive organic molecules through bottom-up approaches, such as hydrothermal approaches, microwave pyrolysis, and solvothermal methods [11]. Among bottom-up methods, hydrothermal CD synthesis is broadly accepted because of the mild reaction conditions, eco-friendliness, and ease of operation [13]. While the properties of the synthesized CDs mainly depend on the precursors used, considerable attention has been paid to advancing their properties [14]. Biomass carbon-derived CDs have a variety of diverse characteristics; hence, research has focused on developing biomass-derived carbon materials with sufficient performance, activity, and effectiveness for commercialization.

Concerns about environmental pollution have increased with the rapid development of the industry. Iron, which often exists in water in the form of Fe3+, is the most important transition metal for almost all living organisms, including humans, because it is involved in various fundamental metabolic processes. At the same time, high or low iron levels may cause serious health disorders [15,16]. The World Health Organization (WHO) has proposed a safe limit of 5.36 μM for Fe3+ in potable water [17]. Therefore, the development of methods for the fast, selective, and sensitive detection of Fe3+ is very important in the analysis field, especially for environmental applications. To date, several methods have been developed for the determination of Fe3+, including atomic absorption/emission spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometry, electrochemical analysis, fluorimetry, and calorimetry [18]. Among these detection methods, the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ using fluorescent (FL) probes and chemosensors has attracted much attention in recent decades. Significantly, FL CDs are promising competitors to traditional quantum dots as FL probes (nanosensors) due to their distinctive chemical and optical properties, with research focused on developing eco-friendly FL probes for detecting Fe3+ ions.

Here, we report a green synthetic approach that affords CDs with tunable FL that are prepared by a simple hydrothermal process from the biomass of Morus nigra (black mulberry) fruit juice. In general, Morus nigra fruit contains several active phytoconstituents, including cardiac glycosides, saponins, alkaloids, carbohydrates, amino acids, phenolic compounds, and flavonoids [19,20]. The emission properties, quantum yield, stability, solubility, and toxicity of the Morus nigra CDs (M-CDs) were investigated in detail. The low-cost synthesis method reported herein provides an efficient, reliable, and environmentally friendly nanosensor (M-CDs) for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ in aqueous media by “turn-off” (quenching) FLR recognition. In addition, we demonstrate that these M-CDs are effective FL probes for human colon cancer (HTC-116) cell imaging purposes.

The carbon dots were prepared from Morus nigra (black mulberry) using a simple hydrothermal method. Unripe black mulberry fruit was pulverized well with deionized water using a commercial mixer-grinder. The obtained colloidal suspension was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was carefully sealed and subsequently heated at 200 °C for 24 h in a hot-air oven. The autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the hydrothermally carbonized suspension was filtered through a cellulose membrane (pore size: 0.22 µm) to remove larger particles. The prepared M-CDs were stored in a refrigerator at 5 °C until required. Details of the simple hydrothermal carbonization method used to synthesize the M-CDs from Morus nigra fruit are shown in Scheme 1.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern in Fig. 1(a) provides information about the phase purity and crystallization/graphitization of the synthesized M-CDs; it shows a broad, low-intensity diffraction peak centered at a 2θ value of ~ 22.5° with a calculated interlayer spacing of 3.9 Å, consistent with the (002) lattice distances of typical carbon-based nanomaterials [21–23]. The interlayer spacing (3.9 Å) is larger than that of the bulk graphite (002) plane (3.4 Å), indicative of the presence of a turbostratic carbon structure (poor crystallization) resulting from abundant functional groups at the edges of the M-CDs [24,25]. In addition, the XRD pattern does not show any sharp peaks, which confirms the amorphous nature (abundant sp3 disorder) that dominates the graphitic structure, and is possibly due to the presence of numerous oxygen- or nitrogen-containing functional groups at the edges and surfaces of the M-CDs as well as the smaller M-CD crystallites [26].

The quality of the obtained M-CDs, including graphitization and purity, was evaluated by Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 1(b) exhibits two peaks centered at 1345 and 1600 cm−1 that correspond to D and G bands, respectively [27,28]. The D band is derived from the vibrations of sp3 carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite [29,30]. The G band corresponds to the E2g mode and the vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms in 2D hexagonal lattices [31]. The relative ID/IG (sp3/sp2 carbon) ratio can be used to determine the degree of graphitization; ID/IG was determined to be approximately 0.97, which suggests the presence of some structural defects in the prepared graphitic M-CDs that resulted in the almost amorphous character of the M-CDs [32]. In addition, the relative areas of the D and G bands can also determine the degree of graphitization/crystallization. The areal D-to-G-band ratio of the M-CDs was found to be 1.8, consistent with poor graphitization (Fig. S1), which is possibly due to the edge effect, smaller domains, and abundant M-CD surface functional groups [33]. This finding is in fair agreement with the XRD data for the M-CDs.

The surface functional moieties of the prepared M-CDs were assessed by Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy, the results of which are shown in Fig. S2. The broad absorption band at 3400–3200 cm−1 corresponds to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations of surface hydroxyl and amino groups, respectively, that are responsible for the hydrophilic nature of the M-CDs [34], whereas the weak absorption bands at 2870 and 2928 cm−1 are ascribable to symmetric and asymmetric C–H stretching vibrations, respectively. Sharp bands corresponding to CO (carboxyl/carbonyl) and CC stretching vibrations are observed at 1670 and 1580 cm−1, respectively [35–37]. The intense CO vibrational band reveals the presence of a large number of carboxyl groups in the prepared M-CDs [38]. The absorption bands at 1450 and 1290 cm−1 correspond to C–N and C–OH groups, respectively, the peaks at 1020–1120 cm−1 signify the presence of C–O–C groups [39], and the band at 1391 cm−1 is attributable to O–H and N–H bending vibrations. The absorption band centered at around 680 cm−1 confirms the existence of out-of-plane C–H stretching vibrations that are due to the formation of carboxylic acid groups on the M-CDs surface [40]. ATR-FTIR spectroscopy revealed that the M-CDs are predominantly composed of abundant carbon-, nitrogen-, and oxygen-containing moieties, with carboxyl, hydroxyl, and nitrile functional groups on their surface. These surface functional groups significantly improve the solubility of M-CDs in water and facilitate applications in biology and biomedicine [40]. Furthermore, the surface functional moieties, chemical composition, and elemental state of the obtained M-CDs were examined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectrum ( Fig. 2(a)) shows that the synthesized M-CDs are composed primarily of carbon (C 1s, 83%), nitrogen (N 1s, 4%), and oxygen (O 1s, 13%). Meanwhile, the absence of other residual elements confirms that the prepared M-CDs are pure. The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2(b)) exhibits five peaks at 284.7, 285.8, 286.5, 287.8, and 288.9 eV that correspond to sp2 and sp3 graphitic structure (CC/C–C), hydroxyl groups bonded to carbon (C–OH), pyridinic-nitrogen functionalities/ epoxy groups (C–N–C/C–O–C), carbonyl groups (CO), and carboxyl groups (OC–OH), respectively [41–43]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2(c)) was deconvoluted into three peaks at binding energies of 399.3, 400.2, and 401.3 eV that assigned to pyrridinic-nitrogen (C–N–C), pyrrolic-nitrogen (C–N–H), and graphitic-nitrogen (C3 N) bonds, respectively [44,45]. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the O 1s spectrum exhibits four peaks at binding energies of 531.2, 532.2, 533.1, and 534.0 eV that attributable to the surface or edge CO, C–OH, C–O–C, and OC–OH/H–O–H groups, respectively [46,47]. These results suggest that the surface of the obtained M-CDs are functionalized with nitrile, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, which endow the M-CDs with hydrophilicity; consequently, they readily disperse in water. In addition, the XPS data are in good agreement with the ATR-FTIR results.

The surface morphology and elemental composition of the prepared M-CDs were further characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). Fig. S3(a–e) shows FESEM images of the prepared M-CDs at various magnifications. M-CDs are smooth, with a continuous sheet-like morphology that is possibly ascribable to their surface functionalities and smaller particles (domains). EDS results are shown in Fig. 3, confirming the elemental composition and surface functionalities of the M-CDs. The EDS maps show that the M-CDs are composed of uniformly distributed elemental carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen (overlapping image, Fig. 3(e)). M-CD morphology was characterized by transmittance electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The TEM images in Fig. 4(a) show that the M-CDs appear to be almost spherical and highly monodispersed. M-CDs are predominantly distributed in the 3–6 nm size range and have a mean diameter of 4.5 nm, based on Gaussian particle-size-distribution fitting (inset Fig. 4(a)). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 4(b and c)) show that the M-CDs exhibit fair crystallinity (graphitization), with the M-CD lattice spacing determined to be 0.21 nm, which is close to the reported in-plane lattice spacing of the graphitic carbon/graphene (100) plane (0.21 nm) [48,49].

The optical properties of the prepared M-CDs were examined by Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy and FLR spectroscopy. A prepared M-CDs solution exhibits a strong absorption peak at ~280 nm and a shoulder at around 320 nm ( Fig. 5(a)) that correspond to aromatic π–π* transition domains (CC bonds) and n–π* transitions (i.e., CO and CN bonds), respectively [50]. The prepared M-CDs are effortlessly dispersed in water to form a nearly clear yellow dispersion in daylight that emits cyan-blue FLR under UV light (inset Fig. 5(a)). Fig. S4(a) shows the maximum excitation spectrum of a prepared aqueous M-CDs dispersion at a wavelength of 360 nm. Furthermore, FLR spectra were recorded with 10-nm excitation-wavelength increments in the 300–460 nm range. The FLR emission maximum became redshifted (from 400 to 500 nm) as the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 nm to 460 nm, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The FLR intensity increased as the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 and 360 nm, and then gradually decreased as the wavelength was further increased from 360 to 460 nm. Excitation and emission maxima were observed at 360 and 427 nm, respectively. The FLR spectrum shows a narrow (90 nm) full-width at half-maximum due to the acceptable degree of graphitization and crystallinity of the M-CDs. Normalized FLR emission spectra are shown in Fig. S4(b), which suggests that the prepared M-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent FLR behavior. This is ascribable to various particle sizes, synergism due to quantum confinement, and functional groups that are still disputed [34,51]. Moreover, the prepared M-CDs emitted tunable FLR with a high quantum yield of 24%.

The long-term stability and photostability of the M-CDs are depicted in Fig. 6. The FLR spectra of the prepared M-CDs were acquired at the time of synthesis and after long-term storage for 100 d. Fig. 6(a) reveals insignificant changes in FLR intensity even after the aqueous M-CDs solution had been stored for 100 d. The photographic images (inset of Fig. 6(a)) show similar FLR emission brightness at day 1 and day 100, which further supports the notion that the M-CDs are long-term stable. Furthermore, M-CDs photostability was examined by exposure to a 4-W 365-nm UV light source; no significant changes in intensity were observed with increasing irradiation time, as shown in Fig. 6(b). The aqueous M-CDs solution retained more than 97% of its initial FLR intensity even after a long period of exposure (100 min); the photographic images (inset of Fig. 6(b)) also show that the M-CDs are photostable. Furthermore, no significant precipitation or peak changes were observed. Hence, these two testing conditions revealed that the M-CDs exhibit excellent long-term stability and photostability, which facilitate bio-labeling/bio-imaging applications [52].

Quenching performance was examined in the presence of various metal ions (1000 μM Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+) to determine the selective FLR-sensing efficiency of the prepared M-CDs toward Fe3+ ( Fig. 7(a)). Compared to the other metal ions, almost zero M-CDs FLR was observed in the presence of Fe3+, as shown in Fig. 7(a). The FLR response graph (Fig. 7(b)) shows that the M-CDs are highly selective toward Fe3+ over the other metal ions. The FLR intensity ratios (F/F0) of M-CDs in the presence of other interfering metal ions, such as Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+, did not show any obvious changes at a concentration of 1000 μM. At this concentration, Cu2+ and Fe2+ quenched the FLR emission by ~30% and 31%, respectively, while Fe3+ quenched it by 97%.

We next investigated the effect of Fe3+ concentration on the sensing system. The M-CDs initially emitted strong FLR, which decreased in intensity with increasing Fe3+ concentration, as shown in Fig. 7(c). Fig. 7(d) reveals that relative fluorescence (F0/F, where F0 and F are the FLR intensities in the absence (blank) and presence of Fe3+, respectively) and the Fe3+ concentration are linearly related in the 5–30 μM range (R2 = 0.998). Based on the linear relationship, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.47 μM using Eq. (1):
(1)
LOD =
3 σ
S
where σ and S are the standard deviation (n = 6) and slope of the linear calibration plot, respectively. The LOD for the detection of Fe3+ in this study (0.47 μM) is much lower than the WHO-proposed Fe3+ limit (5.36 μM), which suggests that the synthesized M-CDs are promising candidates for the detection of trace amounts of Fe3+ [17]. As shown in Table 1, the Fe3+ detection limit of the synthesized M-CDs is acceptable when compared to reported detection limits for Fe3+, further indicating that the synthesized M-CDs are excellent candidates for the detection of Fe3+ [13,53–61].

Fig. 8 shows photographic images of aqueous M-CDs solutions with various metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+) under normal light (daylight) and UV light (365 nm). Aqueous M-CDs solution in the presence of all metal ions emitted bright cyan-blue FLR under UV light in a manner that can be visualized by the naked eye. This photographic image vividly shows that M-CDs FLR is quenched by an aqueous Fe3+ solution, revealing that the M-CDs are very suitable candidates for the selective detection of Fe3+. A competitive experiment was performed in the presence of the other common metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+) to evaluate the specific selectivity of the sensor toward Fe3+ in a complex real environmental system (Fig. S5(a)). First, 500 μM Fe3+ alone was added to the aqueous M-CDs solution, and the FLR emission spectrum was recorded. The FLR emission intensities of aqueous M-CDs solution in the presence of Fe3+ (500 μM) and other metal ions (500 μM), including Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+ were next examined. As shown in Fig. S5(a), none of the other coexisting metal ions led to a significant change in the FLR intensity under the same conditions. The corresponding selective FLR response graph (Fig. S5(b)) shows that the M-CDs are highly specific toward Fe3+, even in the presence of other ions under the same conditions. The specific selectivity of the M-CDs toward Fe3+ is possibly due to strong interactions (coordination) between Fe3+ and surface functionalities, such as the nitrile, hydroxyl, and carboxylic groups of the M-CDs; these strong interactions can interrupt the radiative-transition process, leading to M-CD FLR quenching [16,62,63].

Furthermore, M-CDs FLR quenching in the presence of Fe3+ was investigated by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy; spectra of the prepared M-CD aqueous solutions before and after the addition of Fe3+ are shown in Fig. S6. The spectrum of the aqueous M-CDs solution exhibits absorption peaks at around 280 and 320 nm in the absence of Fe3+ that are ascribable to CC (π–π*) and CO/CN (n–π*), respectively (details are discussed in Section 3.1). The UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibits a strong, slightly redshifted π–π* absorption peak (285 nm) in the presence of Fe3+ compared to that of the M-CDs (280 nm), which suggests that the CC bonds of the M-CDs exhibit a new electronic effect due to interactions between Fe3+ and surface functionalities [64]. In addition, the n–π* shoulder peak in the spectrum of the M-CDs is almost absent in the presence of Fe3+ due to coordination between Fe3+ and surface functional groups (CO/CN). Hence, M-CDs FLR quenching is attributable to synergism between oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups that coordinate to Fe3+. To demonstrate FLR quenching in the presence of Fe3+, aqueous M-CDs solutions in the presence and absence of Fe3+ were exposed to daylight and 360-nm UV light, the results of which are shown in the inset of Fig. S6. The photographic images of the aqueous M-CDs solution in the absence of Fe3+ exhibit bright cyan-blue FLR when illuminated by 360-nm UV light, which is almost completely turned off (quenched) in the presence of Fe3+, demonstrating that M-CDs FLR is quenched by Fe3+.

Taken together, the above results confirm that M-CDs FLR is specifically selective toward Fe3+; hence, we believe that the M-CDs are promising candidates for selective Fe3+ sensing in aqueous media. Surface functional groups, such as the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of the prepared M-CDs, are highly sensitive to Fe3+ through coordination. A plausible electron/energy-transfer (ET) FLR quenching mechanism is shown in Fig. 9. Electron-deficient Fe3+ coordinates to the functional groups on the surfaces of the M-CDs, and the half-filled 3d orbital of Fe3+ receives electrons from the M-CD excited state. This intramolecular photoinduced ET leads to M-CDs FLR quenching by non-radiative electron/hole recombination [64,65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applying the synthesized M-CDs to HCT-116 cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: In the field of nanobiotechnology, CDs are gradually but increasingly being used as FL probes for cellular microscopy because they exhibit excellent optical characteristics, including high quantum yields, insignificant photobleaching, and tunable FLR emissions. Hence, to explore potential applications of the M-CDs, we assessed their utility for imaging human colon cancer (HTC-116) cells by confocal microscopy, with cell viability first assessed. Fig. 10 reveals that no significant cytotoxicity, with cell viability greater than 97% recorded after incubation for 24 h, even at a concentration of 200 mg mL−1. The healthy status of the HTC-116 cells confirmed that the M-CDs are very biocompatible with HTC-116 cells. Hence, M-CDs were used as FLR probes to image HTC-116 cells.

Confocal images of M-CDs conjugated with HTC-116 cells for incubation times of 12 and 24 h were acquired using various filters with distinct excitation wavelengths (405, 488, and 555 nm). Fig. 11 reveals that emission signals were not observed in HTC-116 cells in the absence of M-CDs using the above-mentioned filters or in the bright field. The confocal FLR images of the M-CDs-conjugated HTC-116 cells revealed obvious bright colors, namely blue, green, and red, when excited at 405, 488, and 555 nm, respectively. The M-CDs-conjugated HTC-116 cells were slightly brighter when incubated for 24 h than for 12 h. The overlapping images also highlight the bright multicolor emission of M-CDs-conjugated HTC-116 cells, which emit multiple colors due to the ideal excitation-dependent FLR properties of the M-CDs. These results confirm that the M-CDs are successful FL-probe candidates for imaging HTC-116 cells. Furthermore, the results suggest that the M-CDs are efficiently internalized and equally distributed in the entire HTC-116 cell body. This is ascribable to the presence of multiple active functional groups on the M-CDs surfaces, such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, and amine/amide groups. In addition, the Fe3+-conjugated M-CDs used as FL probes to image HTC-116 cells were examined using confocal microscopy at various excitation wavelengths (405, 488, and 555 nm) and in a bright field. The M-CDs+Fe3+-complex-conjugated HTC-116 cells did not show any FL signal even after incubation for 24 h. The absence of the FL signal is ascribable to the presence of Fe3+, which forms an ET complex (M-CDs+Fe3+), resulting in FLR quenching. M-CD FLR is quenched in the presence of Fe3+ for the reasons discussed in Section 3.2. These results demonstrate that the synthesized M-CDs can act as nanocarriers for multicolor imaging, cell labeling, and drug delivery.

M-CDs that exhibit strong FL were successfully prepared by subjecting Morus nigra fruit to facile hydrothermal carbonization. This method requires a relatively mild temperature (200 °C), does not need any special equipment, is energy-efficient, and affords large-scale high-quality M-CDs in one-pot. The as-synthesized fairly graphitized M-CDs were found to be monodispersed with 4.5 nm in size, on average. Aqueous solutions of M-CDs emit bright cyan-blue FLR when irradiate with UV light, with red-shifted emission wavelengths observed when excitation at longer wavelengths (quantum yield of 24%). In addition, the M-CDs are readily dispersible in aqueous media without any coagulation/precipitation due to the presence of nitrogen/oxygen-based groups on their surfaces. Due to the aforementioned properties, the M-CDs act as an attractive FL sensor for the highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ in aqueous media through FLR quenching, with a detection limit of 0.47 μM. The surfaces of the M-CDs are highly functionalized with nitrogen/oxygen-based groups that are complex with Fe3+ to form an ET system that results in FLR quenching. The M-CDs are highly biocompatible with HTC-116 cells, with > 97% viability observed at a (high) concentration of 200 μg mL−1. Confocal FLR images of HTC-116 cells in the presence of the M-CDs revealed bright illumination with multiple colors (blue, green, and red) at excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, and 561 nm. Thus, the present work provides an innovative route for the cost-effective synthesis of versatile and highly FL heteroatom-doped CDs from various types of biomass waste for FL sensing and bio-imaging applications. In addition, such M-CDs are unique and may find many additional applications in the biosensing, biomedical, and environmental fields.

Raji Atchudan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel Edison: Methodology, Resources, Validation. Suguna Perumal: Writing – review & editing. Rajangam Vinodh: Data curation, Formal analysis. Ashok K. Sundramoorthy: Visualization, Validation. Rajendran Suresh Babu: Formal analysis, Validation. Yong Rok Lee: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government: MSIT (2021R1A2B5B02002436).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.128073.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhu, D. Shen, Q. Liu, C. Wu, S. Gu, Sustainable synthesis of bright green fluorescent carbon quantum dots from lignin for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions, Applied Surface Science, 565 (2021) 150526.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted green synthesis of ﬂuorescent carbon quantum dots from Mexican Mint extract for Fe3+ detection and bio-imaging applications
Environ. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Architha, M. Ragupathi, C. Shobana, T. Selvankumar, P. Kumar, Y.S. Lee, R. Kalai Selvan, Microwave-assisted green synthesis of ﬂuorescent carbon quantum dots from Mexican Mint extract for Fe3+ detection and bio-imaging applications, Environmental Research, 199 (2021) 111263.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mint leaf derived carbon dots for dual analyte detection of Fe(iii) and ascorbic acid
RSC Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Raveendran, A.R. Suresh Babu, N.K. Renuka, Mint leaf derived carbon dots for dual analyte detection of Fe(iii) and ascorbic acid, RSC Advances, 9 (2019) 12070–12077.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile green and one-pot synthesis of seville orange derived carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ ions
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Senol, E. Bozkurt, Facile green and one-pot synthesis of seville orange derived carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ ions, Microchemical Journal, 159 (2020) 105357.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doping enhanced fluorescent carbon dots: green synthesis and their applications for bioimaging and label-free detection of Au3+ ions
ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liao, Z. Cheng, L. Zhou, Nitrogen-Doping Enhanced Fluorescent Carbon Dots: Green Synthesis and Their Applications for Bioimaging and Label-Free Detection of Au3+ Ions, ACS Sustainable Chemistry & Engineering, 4 (2016) 3053–3061.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green preparation of versatile nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from watermelon juice for cell imaging, detection of Fe3+ ions and cysteine, and optical thermometry
J. Mol. Liq.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Lu, Y. Duan, Y. Song, J. Tan, L. Zhou, Green preparation of versatile nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from watermelon juice for cell imaging, detection of Fe3+ ions and cysteine, and optical thermometry, Journal of Molecular Liquids, 269 (2018) 766–774.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyvinyl alcohol enhanced fluorescent sulfur quantum dots for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ and temperature in cells
Part. Part. Syst. Charact.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Lei, Z. Huang, P. Gao, J. Sun, L. Zhou, Polyvinyl Alcohol Enhanced Fluorescent Sulfur Quantum Dots for Highly Sensitive Detection of Fe3+ and Temperature in Cells, Particle & Particle Systems Characterization, 38 (2021) 2000332.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent carbon dots as a potential fluorescence probe for selective sensing of ferric ions in aqueous solution
Chemosensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Atchudan, S.C. Kishore, T.N.J.I. Edison, S. Perumal, R. Vinodh, A.K. Sundramoorthy, R.S. Babu, M. Alagan, Y.R. Lee, Highly Fluorescent Carbon Dots as a Potential Fluorescence Probe for Selective Sensing of Ferric Ions in Aqueous Solution, Chemosensors, 9 (2021) 301.
